channel.title: NPR Topics: Science
channel.description: The latest health and science news. Updates on medicine, healthy living, nutrition, drugs, diet, and advances in science and technology. Subscribe to the Health & Science podcast.
channel.copyright: Copyright 2024 NPR - For Personal Use Only

item.title: The Little Probe That Could: Why Voyager 1 Matters, and Why NASA Just Switched Part of It Off
item.description: This week, NASA announced it had shut down one of that spacecraft's remaining science instruments — not because the mission has failed, but to keep it alive a little longer.
item.content:encoded:
This week, NASA announced it had shut down one of that spacecraft's remaining science instruments — not because the mission has failed, but to keep it alive a little longer.

(Image credit: NASA)
item.dc:creator: Willem Marx

item.title: Photos: How overfishing in Southeast Asia is an ecological and human crisis
item.description: A rare look at one of the world's most critical and understudied environmental crises. Southeast Asia produces more than half of the world's fish, yet its waters are among the most depleted and contested.
item.content:encoded:
A rare look at one of the world's most critical and understudied environmental crises. Southeast Asia produces more than half of the world's fish, yet its waters are among the most depleted and contested.

(Image credit: Nicole Tung)
item.dc:creator: Nicole Tung

item.title: How a Japanese poet's diary helps scientists reconstruct solar cycles
item.description: Researchers used a Japanese poet's diary to track solar events that took place over 800 years ago.
item.content:encoded: Researchers used a Japanese poet's diary to track solar events that took place over 800 years ago.
item.dc:creator: Scott Simon

item.title: This week in science: Small talk, more human lobster killing, and an ancient flood
item.description: NPR's Short Wave team talks about the surprising benefits of small talk, more humane ways to kill lobsters, and an ancient flood that may have helped create the Grand Canyon.
item.content:encoded: NPR's Short Wave team talks about the surprising benefits of small talk, more humane ways to kill lobsters, and an ancient flood that may have helped create the Grand Canyon.
item.dc:creator: Emily Kwong

item.title: How seals' whiskers make them master underwater hunters
item.description: Their sensitive facial hair may be the harbor seals superpower for tracking fish, scientists are learning.
item.content:encoded:
Their sensitive facial hair may be the harbor seals superpower for tracking fish, scientists are learning.

(Image credit: Robin Heinrich/Marine Science Center)
item.dc:creator: Ari Daniel

item.title: New species of glass frog identified in Ecuador
item.description: A new type of glass frog has been discovered in Ecuador, and researchers have named it after weightlifter Neisi Dajomes, the first Ecuadorian woman to win an Olympic gold medal.
item.content:encoded: A new type of glass frog has been discovered in Ecuador, and researchers have named it after weightlifter Neisi Dajomes, the first Ecuadorian woman to win an Olympic gold medal.
item.dc:creator: Gurjit Kaur

item.title: In the brain, objects seen and imagined follow the same neural path
item.description: New evidence finds that sight and imagination rely on the same neurons and use the same neural code.
item.content:encoded:
New evidence finds that sight and imagination rely on the same neurons and use the same neural code.

(Image credit: Marco Bottigelli)
item.dc:creator: Jon Hamilton

item.title: What a chimpanzee 'civil war' can teach us about how societies fall apart
item.description: A long-term study of the world's largest known community of chimpanzees has documented a rare event: what the researchers describe as the primate equivalent of a "civil war."
item.content:encoded:
A long-term study of the world's largest known community of chimpanzees has documented a rare event: what the researchers describe as the primate equivalent of a "civil war."

(Image credit: Aaron Sandel)
item.dc:creator: Nathan Rott

item.title: The real space science behind 'Project Hail Mary'
item.description: The science fiction blockbuster wowed audiences with its depiction of space travel and more. Here's what NASA staff and other scientists say about the basis for the amazing events of the film.
item.content:encoded:
The science fiction blockbuster wowed audiences with its depiction of space travel and more. Here's what NASA staff and other scientists say about the basis for the amazing events of the film.

(Image credit: Jonathan Olley)
item.dc:creator: Tara Haelle

item.title: After a whirlwind mission to the moon, astronauts are back home. Here's what's next
item.description: The Orion crew module containing the four Artemis II astronauts splashed down in the Pacific Ocean Friday evening.
item.content:encoded:
The Orion crew module containing the four Artemis II astronauts splashed down in the Pacific Ocean Friday evening.

(Image credit: Bill Ingalls)
item.dc:creator: Brendan Byrne
